data.title: Trump spoke with Venezuela’s Maduro, discussed meeting in US, report says
data.description:
US President Donald Trump spoke by phone last week with leftist Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro, with the two rivals discussing a possible meeting in the United States, The New York Times reported on Friday.
The reported call comes as Trump’s administration piles pressure on Venezuela with a major military deployment in the Caribbean that includes the world’s largest aircraft carrier.
Washington says the aim is curbing drug trafficking but Caracas insists regime change is the ultimate goal.
The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment about the Times report, which said Secretary of State Marco Rubio was also in on the call.
US forces have carried out strikes against more than 20 alleged Venezuelan drug-smuggling vessels in the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific Ocean since early September, killing more than 80 people.

Washington has yet to release evidence that the vessels it targeted were used to smuggle drugs or posed a threat to the United States.
And regional tensions have flared as a result of the campaign and the accompanying military build-up.
The report about the Trump-Maduro call comes a day after the US president said efforts to halt Venezuelan drug trafficking by land were imminent, further ratcheting up tensions with Caracas.
“You probably noticed that people aren’t wanting to be delivering by sea, and we’ll be starting to stop them by land,” he said in remarks to US troops, adding: “Also the land is easier, but that’s going to start very soon.”

data.title: China’s extended property slump was always expected
data.description:
History does not repeat itself, as the saying goes, but it often rhymes. That very much depends on the kind of history being considered. If you are talking about real estate and the stock market, history absolutely repeats itself, almost like an eternal recurrence. Maybe it’s because greed and fear are ingrained in human nature. The claim that “this time is different” has been called the four most dangerous words in investment.
Many people have been so frustrated and hurt by the prolonged slump in the Chinese property market that any glimmer of hope is worth latching onto. So when John Lam, UBS Group AG’s head of China property research and a frequent contrarian, boldly made a bullish call on the Chinese property market last year, some people cheered.
Lam earned a good deal of street cred in early 2021 by correctly downgrading the now-liquidated China Evergrande Group – often described as the world’s most indebted developer – almost a year before it defaulted on its debt.
Sadly, he has now rejoined the pessimistic consensus of his peers and predicted at least another two years before recovery for the depressed residential property market, now in its fourth year of downturn. “People who bought homes in the past decade may all be loss-making,” he told Bloomberg.
Those who have studied the aftermath of the bursting of giant property bubbles won’t be surprised. China’s prolonged slump is not the exception but the rule.


In his classic Manias, Panics, and Crashes: A History of Financial Crises, the late economic historian Charles Kindleberger examined the history of real estate in Chicago and Japan as being paradigmatic of the recurrent rise and fall of the property sector: the bigger and longer the bubble, the worse the economic fallout. The depressed market that follows tends to be prolonged.
Kindleberger cites the influential thesis first developed by economist Homer Hoyt in his 1933 doctoral dissertation, “One Hundred Years of Land Values in Chicago”, which was published as a book the same year. Hoyt traced five cycles of boom and bust, as well as their substantial damage to the city’s economic development. Very early on, Chicago was a byword for land speculation. After the German victory in the Franco-Prussian War of 1870-1871, leading to German unification, property speculation took off in Berlin and Vienna, such that Berlin was dubbed “Chicago on the [river] Spree”.
It was once assumed that mortgage holders (borrowers) and banks (lenders) would be more prudent and responsible than stock speculators (who borrow on margins) and stock brokers (who lend them) because the former group had a much longer time horizon. That, of course, was rarely the case. Property speculation, as Hongkongers and mainlanders know well, can be much worse and more damaging than stock market mania.

With respect to Japanese real estate, Kindleberger quotes a price index for residential property in six large cities, starting with 100 in 1955, reaching 4,100 in the mid-1970s, 5,800 in about 1980 and then rising to a peak of 20,600 in 1989. The twin collapses of the Japanese property and stock markets resulted in the so-called lost decades, causing not only economic and political upheavals but tearing at the country’s social fabric.
China’s case is both worse and better, different but also the same. It has long been observed that the Chinese stock market moves to its own tune and does not necessarily track the overall economy.
Not so for the property market. Historically, the real estate sector accounted for 25 to 30 per cent of China’s GDP. A severe and prolonged downturn in one must adversely affect the other. In China, the wealth of the urban middle class is overwhelmingly tied up in property, making the transition to a consumption-driven economy even more difficult.


However, unlike most other property market collapses, the Chinese bubble was deliberately punctured by the central government. Under President Xi Jinping, Beijing was willing to bite the bullet rather than kick the can down the road. Not only were local officials and banks barred from bailing out developers, the most indebted ones were compelled to fail.
In that sense, the Chinese leadership has painfully addressed the perennial problem of moral hazard – the “too big to fail” conundrum – by making sure the current generation, rather than future ones, suffer for their own excesses.
That doesn’t make the havoc induced any less severe, though. If Lam of UBS and his fellow analysts are right, the pain might yet last longer.

data.title: Andre Agassi on Hong Kong, his comeback and learning to live in the present
data.description:
If there’s one piece of advice Andre Agassi offers, it’s to dream while awake. “It’s way too easy to dream when you’re sleeping,” says the American tennis legend. “Don’t be scared to dream big because it takes as much effort to dream big as it does small.” He advises choosing your definition of success carefully. Even if you achieve it, it might not feel the way you expected.
Fresh off his run as Laver Cup captain, Agassi was in Hong Kong in October for the Prudential NextGen Aces event, a panel series and exhibition match. It was a return to a city that changed the course of his career. He has fond memories of 1999, when he won the Salem Open against Boris Becker. “Hong Kong was definitely a good-luck charm for me in 1999,” he says. “It started me off on a path of winning, and [it] didn’t seem like I stopped for a while.”

That year marked a defining comeback: from dropping from his No 1 position in 1995 to No 141 in 1997, Agassi clawed his way back to the top. The Hong Kong tournament was the first in a string of victories, including the French Open and US Open.
Falling from the top was a turning point. “It was the moment that required the most resilience in my life, off the court,” he says. The climb back demanded daily discipline and a level of focus he’d never experienced before.
He reflected extensively about navigating these career peaks and lows in Open, his widely praised 2009 autobiography. In it, he famously admitted to hating tennis – not the sport itself but his relationship to it.

Forced into the sport at a young age by his father, what Agassi felt he lacked then and throughout his life, was agency. “Tennis was never really my choice,” he says. At his peak, he felt at his lowest. “I wanted to quit tennis many times, but none more than that moment.” He speaks openly about the self-destructive spiral that followed: two years of what he calls “self-inflicting and destroying myself in a lot of ways”.
When he finally stepped back, Agassi began to see things differently. Most people, he realised, don’t get to choose the circumstances into which they are born. “It doesn’t mean that we can’t take ownership of our life,” he says.
For Agassi, part of that meant shifting focus. In 1994, he founded the Andre Agassi Foundation for Education to support at-risk kids.
The inspiration came from watching an episode of current affairs television show 60 Minutes on charter schools. “There was something I identified with,” he says of the students, many of whom didn’t have choices. “At my lowest point, I took out a US$40 million mortgage and began the fund.”

The growth of his foundation ran in parallel to his rise back up the tennis rankings. He went on to become the first player in the Open era to win all four majors on the three different surfaces: hard, clay and grass. In 2006, plagued by chronic back injuries, he played his final match at the US Open and retired.
Given the volatility of his career and the growth that followed, Agassi has plenty to offer the next generation of athletes, most of it centred on staying present and letting go of what you can’t control. “Don’t let results distract you from the most important part of the process, which is growing and getting better,” he says. “It doesn’t matter if it takes 10 or 50 years to get there, everyone has their process and that growth is a hero’s journey.”
The only thing he really regrets? “Wasted time spent on worrying about my hair.”
Out of all your rivals and opponents, who brought out the best in you?
Probably Patrick Rafter, because his game was the opposite of mine. A bit like Pete (Sampras), but he did it in a way that allowed me to really dial in my shots and get comfortable – Pete could keep me from doing that. I always knew I would play well against Pat.

What was the best part about being a Laver Cup captain?
Getting to know the boys, the players, over the course of not just that week, but the year leading up to it. Understanding how they process things, learning first-hand what helps them be the best of themselves, and hopefully giving them a little something that can help them be even better.
If you could steal any technique or skill from any player, what would it be and from whom?
I would have to go down the road of just pure speed. I mean, court coverage is so crucial, especially these days, it seems like without moving great, you don’t have much of a chance. Moving better would have given me so many more options, with the way I hit the ball, how comfortable I was being in control of a point and the ability to defend. So I would probably take the ability to move like (Carlos) Alcaraz, (Alex) de Minaur or Tommy Paul.
Favourite on-court memory?
Winning the last of the four grand slams, which was the French Open in 1999. It was the one I could have won first, probably a decade earlier, but I ended up never thinking I would have a chance to win it. Then I found myself in the finals and ended up winning it. It felt like a long time coming – I was No 1 in the world, fell to No 141, and climbed back to No 1 by winning the last of the four.

What’s the best advice you’ve received about your game?
Recognising that I don’t have to be great – I just have to be better than one person. Figuring out how to get 100 per cent out of whatever percentage I am that day helped me have the longevity in my career that, at that time, was unprecedented.
What’s the best advice you’ve received in life?
Learning how to live in the present. Not worrying about the future or the past is quite a skill set. Anxiety comes from future-tripping – worrying about something that hasn’t happened – and that’s a very lonely place to live. And regret is living in the past. You spend a lifetime realising the only moment that really seems to matter is the one that’s happening right now.
What’s your favourite stadium to play in?
The Australian Open stadium. The Australian environment and conditions really favoured my game.

If you could play with a player from another generation, who would it be?
It would have to be Alcaraz or (Jannik) Sinner. I never played doubles that much, but if I did, I’d want to partner with these two.

What’s your favourite comfort meal?
It would have to be on the greasy side. My wife (German tennis star Steffi Graf) is on the sweet side, and my cravings are all greasy. I would go with really salty, thin French fries or even nachos with cheese and all sorts of stuff. That would be a decadent, enjoyable meal for me. I’m very much a grease and salt person.

data.title: Trump says all orders signed by Biden with autopen are ‘terminated’
data.description:
Donald Trump said on Friday that all documents signed by autopen under Joe Biden were “terminated”, in a legally uncertain move as the US president escalates attacks on his predecessor.
“Any document signed by Sleepy Joe Biden with the Autopen, which was approximately 92% of them, is hereby terminated, and of no further force or effect,” Trump said on social media.
“I am hereby cancelling all Executive Orders, and anything else that was not directly signed by Crooked Joe Biden, because the people who operated the Autopen did so illegally.”
More to follow …

data.title: Hezbollah leader leaves open possibility of new war with Israel
data.description:
The head of Lebanese militant group Hezbollah said on Friday it retained the right to respond to Israel’s killing days ago of its top military commander and left open the possibility of a new conflict with Israel.
Naim Qassem spoke in a televised address as fears grew in Lebanon that Israel could escalate its bombardment of the country to compel Hezbollah to relinquish its arsenal across the country, which the group has repeatedly rejected.
Israel’s killing of Hezbollah’s top military commander Haytham Ali Tabtabai in a strike on Beirut’s southern suburbs on November 23 sharpened those worries.
Qassem said the group would “set the timing” for any retaliation, and said threats of a broader air campaign had no impact on the group – but that renewed war was possible.
“Do you expect a war later? It’s possible sometime. Yes, this possibility is there, and the possibility of no war is also there,” Qassem said.

Qassem did not explicitly say what the group’s position would be in a new war but said Lebanon should prepare a plan to confront Israel that relies on “its army and its people”.
Qassem also said he hoped Pope Leo’s coming visit to Lebanon “will play a role in bringing about peace and ending the [Israeli] aggression.”
Lebanon is under growing pressure from both Israel and the United States to more swiftly disarm Hezbollah and other militant groups across the country.
Moments after Qassem’s speech ended, Israeli military spokesman Avichay Adraee said the Lebanese army’s efforts to seize Hezbollah weapons in the country’s south were “inadequate”.
“Hezbollah continues to manipulate them and work covertly to maintain its arsenal,” Adraee said in a social media post.
But Hezbollah has said it is unwilling to let go of its arms as long as Israel continues its strikes on Lebanese territory and its occupation of five points in the country’s south.

data.title: 8 of the best new K-dramas to watch in December 2025, including Made in Korea on Disney+
data.description:
It looks like 2025 is saving the best for last when it comes to K-dramas, with global streamers scheduling many of their heavy hitters for December.
Read on to find out what to catch over the next month.
1. The Price of Confession
Lead cast: Kim Go-eun, Jeon Do-yeon, Park Hae-soo


Memories of the Sword co-stars Kim Go-eun and Jeon Do-yeon reunite for the thriller The Price of Confession, which has been generating buzz in the industry thanks to scripts penned by Kwon Jong-kwan, the filmmaker known for Sad Movie and Proof of Innocence.
Directing the series is Crash Landing on You director Lee Jung-hyo.
Jeon plays an art teacher whose peaceful life is overturned following the murder of her husband, a crime she is accused of. While in jail, she meets the mysterious Mo-eun (Kim), and they strike a dangerous deal.
Park Hae-soo (Squid Game season one) plays the prosecutor who investigates the women. (Netflix, December 5)
2. Surely Tomorrow
Lead cast: Park Seo-joon, Won Ji-an


Park Seo-joon (Itaewon Class) stars as entertainment reporter Lee Gyeong-do in the romantic comedy Surely Tomorrow. Joining him on screen is Won Ji-an (Squid Game season two) as Seo Ji-woo, Gyeong-do’s ex-lover from their university theatre group.
Although their relationship ended, they got back together eight years later, only to sever ties once more. Now, Gyeong-do is working on the story of a scandal involving Ji-woo’s husband, bringing the pair back together again.
Surely Tomorrow is the latest series from writer Yoo Young-ah (Divorce Attorney Shin, Thirty-Nine). (JTBC/Prime Video, December 6)
3. Pro Bono
Lead cast: Jung Kyung-ho


Hospital Playlist star Jung Kyung-ho plays a lawyer for the second time this year, following Oh My Ghost Clients, in the drama Pro Bono. He takes on the role of Kang Da-wit, an ambitious judge moving up the ladder fast, until a controversial case leads to his resignation.
Da-wit regroups and decides to become a human rights lawyer at a non-profit firm known as Pro Bono. There, he joins forces with the idealist Park Ki-bbeum (So Ju-yeon, The Midnight Romance in Hagwon) to help people in desperate situations.
Pro Bono is directed by Kim Sung-yoon (Itaewon Class) and written by Moon Yoo-seok (The Devil Judge). (tvN, December 6)
4. Villains
Lead cast: Yoo Ji-tae, Kwak Do-won, Lee Beom-soo, Lee Min-jung

Yoo Ji-tae (Vigilante) leads the series Villains as the criminal mastermind “J”, who never fails to pull off his daring crimes. Kwak Do-won (The Wailing) appears as Jang Joon-hyeok, a corrupt detective who takes bribes and steals from thieves.
Lee Beom-soo (Mercy for None) plays Cha Ki-tae, who used to head the financial crime team at the National Intelligence Service (NIS) until his life went topsy-turvy thanks to one of J’s ploys.
Genius counterfeit artist Han Soo-hyun (Lee Min-jung, Once Again) bides her time and uses her “super notes” to exact revenge on J. (TVing/HBO Max, December 18)
5. Love Me
Lead cast: Seo Hyun-jin, Chang Ryul


Seo Hyun-jin (The Trunk) leads the cast of the melodrama Love Me as Seo Joon-kyung, a single woman working as an OB-GYN. Despite appearances, things are far from perfect, and she lives a lonely life, harbouring a dark secret from her past.
Her family collapsed seven years ago, which caused her to run away and never look back. She eventually meets a man who allows her to open up about her past and loneliness.
Love Me is directed by Jo Young-min, who made the acclaimed series You and Everything Else earlier this year. (JTBC, December 19)
6. I Dol I
Lead cast: Choi Soo-young, Kim Jae-young


Girls’ Generation member Choi Soo-young (Second Shot at Love) plays Maeng Se-na, a star lawyer known for taking on high-profile criminal cases, for which she has been dubbed “lawyer for villains”.
Se-na also has a secret: she is a huge fan of K-pop star Do Ra-ik (Kim Jae-young, The Judge from Hell). Her professional life and personal passion suddenly collide when she takes on a new case defending Ra-ik against a murder accusation. (ENA, December 22)
7. Made in Korea
Lead cast: Hyun Bin, Jung Woo-sung


Hyun Bin and Woo Min-ho, the director of Harbin, join forces again for the political thriller Made in Korea.
Set in the turbulent 1970s, when Korea was ruled by military dictator Park Chung-hee, the show focuses on the ambitious Baek Ki-tae (Hyun) and the tenacious Jang Gun-young (Jung Woo-sung, 12.12: The Day).
This high-budget series has already been renewed for a second season, which is currently in production and expected to drop in late 2026. (Disney+, December 24)
8. Cashero
Lead cast: Lee Jun-ho, Kim Hye-jun

Based on a webcomic of the same name, Cashero tells the story of Kang Sang-woong (Lee Jun-ho, Typhoon Family), a public servant at a community centre who suddenly develops a superpower.
His power is superhuman strength, but it only works in proportion to how much money he has: he becomes strong when he has money, and when he uses his strength, he becomes broke.
Kim Hye-jun (A Shop for Killers) plays his level-headed girlfriend Kim Min-sook, while Kim Byung-chul (Doctor Cha) and Kim Hyang-gi (Along with the Gods) appear as other characters with unusual superpowers. (Netflix, December 26)

data.title: Christmas 2025 gift guide: children’s looks from Ugg, Uniqlo and more
data.description:
The holiday season is well and truly upon us. Thankfully, our expertly curated gift guide is here to charm even the choosiest recipients on your Christmas list. From refined wardrobe essentials for the unfailingly well-dressed to little indulgences for the littlest ones, here are some of the finest gifts to give (or hopefully receive) this Christmas.
Uniqlo beanie

Thom Browne pullover

Furla bag

Loro Piana cuddly toy

Wheat dress

Ugg Kids clogs

data.title: China halts soy imports from Brazil plants, pivots to US amid food safety probe
data.description:
China has halted imports of soybeans from five Brazilian exporters after inspectors found pesticide-treated wheat mixed into a cargo bound for Beijing, officials from both countries said on Thursday.
The suspension by China’s General Administration of Customs was communicated to Brazil’s agriculture ministry on Wednesday and made public on Thursday.
The ban covers two Cargill facilities and one plant each run by Louis Dreyfus Co., CHS Agronegocios and 3Tentos Agroindustrial. Four of the plants are in São Paulo state. One is in Rio Grande do Sul.
Inspectors in China discovered the contamination during checks at a port. The ship Shine Ruby was carrying about 69,000 tonnes of soybeans. Inside the hold, they found roughly ten tonnes of wheat treated with a chemical coating not authorised for food or animal feed.


The customs agency called it a “serious violation” of Chinese food safety rules. It said the pesticide involved is only approved for seed treatment and is considered toxic if consumed. Wheat from Brazil is not cleared for import to China, adding to the breach.
In a letter to the Brazilian embassy in Beijing, the agency said the decision was made to protect public health and ensure the safety of imports. The order took effect on Thursday and applies only to the listed facilities. Other plants run by the same companies remain cleared to export.
Cargill, Louis Dreyfus, CHS and 3Tentos did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The action came as China increased purchases of American soybeans after new trade talks between Washington and Beijing.
The US Department of Agriculture reported this week that Chinese buyers signed contracts for at least ten to twelve shipments of soybeans from the United States, valued at about US$300 million. Each cargo carries around 60,000 tonnes and is set to sail in January from Gulf and Pacific northwest ports.
The deals followed a phone call on Monday between US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, during which Trump asked Xi to increase and speed up farm purchases and that Xi “more or less agreed”.
Washington said China has committed to buy 12 million tonnes of US soybeans this year, rising to 25 million tonnes in the coming years. Analysts said the timing fits a seasonal pattern. Brazil’s exports slow during its planting season, giving US suppliers a short window to fill orders.
Despite that uptick, Brazil remains China’s main source of soybeans. Between January and October, Brazil exported 94 million tonnes of the crop, including about 73 million tonnes to China.
In Brasilia, the agriculture ministry said on Friday it had received China’s notice and called the affected companies for a review.
“Brazil maintains high standards of plant health and food safety,” the ministry said in a statement, adding it would work with Chinese officials to resolve the issue.
The suspended facilities account for less than 0.1 per cent of Brazil’s export capacity. Officials expect soybean exports to China to exceed 100 million tonnes this year.

China said the ban will remain until the companies complete investigations into the contamination and adopt corrective measures verified by Brazilian authorities.
Customs officials reminded Brazil that similar problems occurred in December last year and in January, when pesticide-coated soybeans were detected in separate shipments. Those suspensions were lifted after Brazil introduced corrective measures in April.
China is Brazil’s largest agricultural customer, buying more than three-quarters of its soybean exports. The trade was worth over 31 billion reais this year.

data.title: Death toll from Hong Kong fire climbs to 128 as city prepares for mourning period
data.description:
The death toll from Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in seven decades climbed to more than 100 on Friday as the government announced a three-day official mourning period from Saturday for victims of the blaze.
National and Hong Kong flags at all its buildings and facilities will fly at half-mast until Monday, with officials cancelling all non-essential public engagements and government-endorsed celebratory events delayed or cancelled.
Residents can also pen their condolences in books placed across the city’s 18 districts.
Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung on Friday afternoon revealed the latest figure of 128 dead from the fire and warned of more bodies being pulled from the scene while 200 were still deemed to be missing.
The city marked the grim milestone with more outpouring of donations and volunteers fanning out to help while authorities swooped in on more suspects over the faulty renovation work at Wang Fuk Court that sparked the inferno.
Anti-corruption officers searched several offices throughout the day and eight people were arrested, bringing the total number under arrest to 11.
As search and rescue efforts continued for the third day on Friday and groups of those displaced made their way to new temporary homes, the government also raised the sum it would give to each family of the deceased victims to HK$200,000 (US$25,690), up from HK$150,000.

Leading the official mourning period, city leader John Lee Ka-chiu, along with senior officials and cabinet members, will hold three minutes of silence at 8am on Saturday.
During the three days, a book of condolence will be placed across the city’s 18 districts. Residents can sign it between 9am and 9pm.
Secretary Tang warned of more bodies being found as officers began combing through the singed structures of the seven blocks in the Tai Po estate that had been burning for 43 hours since Wednesday before the flames were fully extinguished early Friday.
Of the deceased, 108 were found at the scene, while four people died at hospital. Another 16 “charred” bodies remained inside the buildings, according to Tang.
“Among the deceased, 39 have been identified. As for the remainder, about half were discovered inside their flats, so we believe we will be able to confirm their identities soon,” said the security minister.
Including the ones found in flats, 89 bodies were deemed not immediately identifiable, suggesting they had been burned beyond recognition, Tang said.
As for the injured, the number had risen to 79, including 12 firefighters.
Among the missing, some were cases of overlapping queries and authorities were still working to resolve them, he added.
The inferno that started on Wednesday afternoon at the tightly packed Tai Po residential complex soon became the worst fire in terms of fatalities since 1957, when a Kowloon tenement blaze claimed 59 lives.
The public is now demanding answers on how the flames were able to spread in mere minutes, why many residents were left unaware until it was too late, and questions of construction materials used in the renovation works that failed safety standards.
Director of Fire Services Andy Yeung Yan-kin confirmed that the fire alarms of all of Wang Fuk Court’s eight buildings had failed to sound during department tests, confirming residents’ earlier reports that they never heard a warning during the blaze.
Yeung added the department would take law enforcement action against the contractor for the failure of its fire safety equipment.
Authorities were blaming highly flammable styrofoam materials as the primary cause for the “unusually fast” and devastating spread of the fire.
Security secretary Tang said the ignited foam panels caused glass to shatter, leading to a swift expansion of the fire and it spread into interior spaces.
“We faced great difficulties in this operation mainly due to the presence of styrofoam along the windows and in the stairwells,” added Yeung.
“When the fire occurred, it caused the flames to spread quickly and into the apartments, causing multiple units to go up in flames at the same time.”
As criminal investigations widened, Hong Kong’s anti-corruption agency, the Independent Commission Against Corruption, made eight more arrests on Friday, including of two consultancy firm directors, Wong Hap-yin and Ng Yeuk of Will Power Architects Company.
Two of the company’s project managers, a pair of owners of a subcontractor for the scaffolding works and a middleman were the others.
On Thursday, three were arrested within the main contractor firm, Prestige Construction & Engineering Co Limited: director and operations manager Ho Kin-yip, 52, director Hau Wa-kin, 54, and safety consultant Steve Wong Chung-kee, 68.
A government report released late Friday night said citywide inspections ordered by the chief executive after the fire on 127 buildings with scaffolding and protective netting had found two using styrofoam boards to cover windows. They were ordered to immediately remove the materials that were deemed the chief culprit of the fire’s spread at the Tai Po estate while legal counsel was being sought on penalties.
The Buildings Department has also issued a directive demanding all registered building professionals and contractors review and report on the fire-retardant documentation for scaffolding nets at all ongoing projects within seven days.
Tang on Friday also called on the city to remain united as he sought to quash “misinformation” circulating online and slammed “rumourmongers” for spreading lies such as how there were no emergency shelters and firefighters lacked supplies.
The government also held a coordination meeting chaired by Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po with all financial regulators including the Monetary Authority, Insurance Authority and Mandatory Provident Fund Schemes Authority to ensure a “compassionate and empathetic approach” for victims.


The institutions were instructed to proactively reach out to affected residents, streamline procedures and expedite claims processing applications, as well as adopt a lenient approach when handling delayed payments.
They were also asked to extend service hours of banks in Tai Po throughout the weekends.
Secretary for Home and Youth Affairs Alice Mak Mei-kuen said those affected by the fire would be receiving additional funding, including HK$200,000 for the families of each of the deceased, along with government assistance throughout the entire bereavement process.
She said the Food and Environmental Hygiene Department would help with crematorium arrangements and columbarium allocation, as well as increase the number of cremation slots. All cremation and funeral services will be covered by the government funding.
An additional HK$50,000 living allowance would also be given to each victim, which will be handed out starting next week.
All of this was on top of the HK$10,000 announced on Thursday, which authorities said was only meant for the immediate emergency situation and that more funds and help were on the way.

Schools in Tai Po were also set to receive either HK$100,000 or HK$50,000.
“This money is meant to help schools in providing flexible aid to students and parents, including for emotional support services, and in replacing their education-related supplies,” said Education minister Christine Choi Yuk-lin.
Private entities, including the Jockey Club, Li Ka Shing Foundation, Jack Ma Foundation and AIA Hong Kong have made combined contributions well exceeding HK$400 million.
Scores of residents lined up at the nearby Kwong Fuk Estate Community Centre to identify bodies of their loved ones via photographs, including beloved pets.
The atmosphere was heavy as many, looking teary-eyed and shaken, consoled one another, while others came bearing flowers.
Tai Po on Friday continued to be a massive, ground-up hub of self-organised, consolidated efforts, as streams of residents and volunteers poured onto a plaza at Kwong Fuk Estate to help with relief operations.
The volunteer ecosystem ran smoothly, with people being guided by hand-drawn maps for designated zones of various types of donations including food, clothing, pet supplies and professional services, as staff mobilised platforms like Telegram and WhatsApp to coordinate efforts.
At the scene were professionals including 29-year-old nurse Gordon Fung, who had set up temporary medical stations to aid affected residents with chronic illnesses and who lost their medication or needed wound cleaning.
Volunteers from the Hong Kong Psychological Society were also stationed at shelters, offering emotional support.
Clinical psychologist Susanna Choy, who was stationed at a shelter , said: “Anyone can talk to us, even for just loss of sleep, we’re here to help.”

data.title: France charges fourth suspect in daring Louvre jewel heist
data.description:
France on Friday charged the fourth alleged member of a four-person gang arrested over last month’s spectacular jewel heist at the Louvre, prosecutors said.
On October 19, the gang raided the world’s most visited art museum in broad daylight, taking just seven minutes to steal jewellery worth an estimated US$102 million before fleeing on scooters.
Paris prosecutor Laure Beccuau described the fourth suspect as a 39-year-old man born in the working-class Paris suburb of Seine-Saint-Denis.
“Already convicted six times, this man was known to courts for various offences, such as pimping, driving without a licence, and receiving stolen goods,” Beccuau said in a statement.
The four suspects believed to have carried out the robbery have now been arrested and charged. They have been charged with organised theft and criminal conspiracy.
The stolen jewellery is still missing.


The last suspected member of the gang was arrested on Tuesday at a construction site in the western French town of Laval, according to a source close to the case.
The other suspects already behind bars – men aged 35, 37 and 39 – are suspected of having been part of the four-person team.
Two of them entered the Apollo Gallery while the other two remained outside, before fleeing together.
A 38-year-old woman who is the partner of one of the men is suspected of complicity. She has been released on bail.
